core.claim: AFF's nuclear licensing reform is a substantial increase in federal regulatory activity
core.reasoning: NRC reform adds mandatory safety certifications, extended site approval windows, and new emergency protocol requirements
core.impact: Plan is topical—increases federal oversight of nuclear energy sector

core.claim: Current US nuclear regulations have stifled new plant construction for 40 years
core.reasoning: No new nuclear plant has come online since 1996 (Watts Bar Unit 1); licensing takes 10-20 years vs 3-5 in France and South Korea
core.impact: US nuclear capacity has flatlined while coal and gas continue providing baseload power

core.claim: Continued fossil fuel dependence causes 350,000 US deaths annually from air pollution
core.reasoning: EPA estimates 350,000 premature deaths from PM2.5 and NOx exposure; nuclear displacement of coal saves lives at scale
core.impact: Every year without nuclear expansion is 350,000 preventable deaths—nuclear is the safest energy source per TWh
core.evidenceSource: EPA Air Quality Report 2023 / Our World in Data Energy Safety

core.claim: Streamlined licensing reduces time-to-operation from 15 to 5 years based on French and Korean models
core.reasoning: France completed 56 reactors in 15 years under its standardized model; Korea's APR-1400 certified in 48 months
core.impact: 5 new plants could come online by 2035 under AFF's reform, displacing 180 GW of fossil capacity

core.claim: Deregulatory reform is not an 'increase' in regulation—AFF removes barriers, not adds them
core.reasoning: Substantially increasing regulation means MORE rules, not FEWER; AFF's streamlining is the opposite of what the resolution requires
core.impact: AFF is non-topical; this is a voting issue

core.claim: Nuclear stagnation is market-driven, not regulatory—investors won't build because costs exceed returns
core.reasoning: Georgia Power's Vogtle Unit 3 cost $17B (4x estimate); investor capital has fled nuclear for cheaper solar and wind
core.impact: Regulatory reform doesn't solve the economic calculus that killed nuclear

core.claim: AFF's air pollution harms are already being solved by renewable energy transition
core.reasoning: Solar and wind are now cheaper than any fossil fuel (LCOE); grid decarbonization is happening without nuclear
core.impact: No unique harm from nuclear inaction if renewables are displacing coal faster than nuclear could
core.evidenceSource: Lazard LCOE Report 2024 / EIA Grid Decarbonization Data

core.claim: French and Korean models are not replicable in the US—supply chain and workforce don't exist
core.reasoning: US has no trained nuclear workforce pipeline; the last welding school closed in 2008; France and Korea maintained continuous construction programs
core.impact: AFF's timeline assumes a skilled workforce that does not exist; 5-year projection is fantasy

core.claim: Small Modular Reactors (SMRs)—AFF's implicit technology—have failed to reach commercial scale anywhere
core.reasoning: NuScale, the only NRC-approved SMR, cancelled its first project in Nov 2023 due to cost overruns; no commercial SMR exists
core.impact: AFF's solvency mechanism is unproven technology; the one real-world test case just failed

core.claim: Regulatory reform is still regulation—adding safety requirements while streamlining process increases federal presence
core.reasoning: AFF adds new audit requirements, standardized safety certifications, and federal site selection authority—net increase in federal regulatory activity
core.impact: T is not a voting issue; AFF clearly within the resolution

core.claim: Vogtle proves regulatory failure, not market failure—cost overruns traced to shifting NRC requirements mid-construction
core.reasoning: Congressional Research Service: 67% of Vogtle overruns attributable to regulatory change orders mid-build, not market dynamics
core.impact: Regulatory certainty solves the investor problem; market will follow if uncertainty is removed

core.claim: Renewables can't provide firm baseload power—nuclear is the only clean firm power source
core.reasoning: Texas winter storm Uri killed 246 people when wind and solar failed; grid reliability requires 24/7 dispatchable clean power
core.impact: Air pollution harms are unique to nuclear pathway—renewables alone cannot decarbonize the grid reliably

core.claim: Workforce can be rebuilt—AFF includes nuclear apprenticeship program funding in the plan
core.reasoning: AFF's plan includes $2B workforce training provision; apprenticeship programs can produce 10,000 trained workers in 5 years
core.impact: Workforce gap is a plan-included answer; NEG ignores this

core.claim: NuScale cancellation is the death knell for SMR economics—Congress won't fund what the market rejected
core.reasoning: NuScale's UAMPS project had DOE backing AND a guaranteed buyer and still couldn't make the numbers work; AFF's $2B workforce fund can't change nuclear economics
core.impact: If NuScale fails with every advantage, AFF's reform fails a fortiori

core.claim: CRS finding is contested—Vogtle overruns began before regulatory changes; first-of-a-kind construction costs are universal
core.reasoning: Every country building its first new reactor in decades faces the same overruns (UK Hinkley Point C: 3x estimate)
core.impact: Regulatory certainty doesn't solve construction cost inflation—this is an industry-wide phenomenon
core.evidenceSource: Hinkley Point C Cost Review 2023 / IAEA Construction Cost Analysis

core.claim: Consolidate NEG Solvency: workforce, SMR failure, and economic calculus are each independent reasons AFF can't solve
core.reasoning: Even if AFF trains workers, they have nothing to build; NuScale is dead; large reactors cost $15-20B each; no utility will commit
core.impact: NEG wins Solvency 3-0: workforce gap, unproven tech, investor flight

core.claim: Large conventional reactors don't need SMRs—AFF's plan works for AP1000 standardized large reactors
core.reasoning: Westinghouse AP1000 is NRC-approved, Korea is building 4 of them right now, Chinese deployments are online
core.impact: NEG's SMR objection is a straw man; AFF never specified SMRs; large standardized reactors are the solvency mechanism

core.claim: Uri proves renewables are insufficient for grid reliability—extend AFF Harms, NEG concedes unique need for firm clean power
core.reasoning: NEG's renewable answer ignores dispatchability problem; AFF's harms stand
core.impact: Harms flow to AFF: 350K deaths, unique nuclear pathway, renewables can't solve alone

core.claim: AP1000 answer: Korea's 4 reactors took 12 years, not 5; US has no standardized construction pipeline
core.reasoning: Korean KEPCO is a state-owned enterprise with 50 years of continuous construction; US private utilities have none of this
core.impact: AFF's AP1000 pivot still requires a workforce and supply chain that doesn't exist in US private market
core.evidenceSource: KEPCO Annual Report 2023 / US Private Nuclear Market Analysis

core.claim: Extend Harms: NEG's 2NR is Solvency-only; Harms is conceded—350K deaths, unique nuclear pathway
core.reasoning: NEG dropped Uri and dispatchability in 2NR; renewable uniqueness answer is gone
core.impact: AFF wins Harms cleanly

core.claim: Solvency: Korean timeline is 12 years but delivers; AFF's 2035 target assumes 10-12 year build—consistent with reality
core.reasoning: AFF never promised 5 years for AP1000; that was 1AC SMR language NEG is misrepresenting; 2035 is achievable
core.impact: Even partial solvency—2 plants by 2035—displaces 40 GW of coal and saves 50,000 lives

questions.admissionNote: AFF conceded French model required continuous construction since 1970s—US hasn't built in 25 years
NEG admitted NuScale had DOE backing—market failure was still total
AFF conceded CRS 67% figure is contested in the literature
